summary.overview: Indiana is experiencing a significant energy affordability crisis, with all five major investor-owned utilities having sought or received rate increases in the past 12–18 months, prompting unprecedented legislative and regulatory action. Governor Braun signed HEA 1002 in February 2026 to restructure rate approvals and mandate low-income assistance programs, while the IURC launched a landmark Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability in March 2026 — the first of its kind in the state. Despite these reforms, consumer advocates and the governor himself have expressed disappointment that the IURC still approved a $71 million AES Indiana rate increase in June 2026, and the OUCC has accused Duke Energy of over-collecting approximately $90 million beyond what regulators authorized.

messaging_recommendations.message: You are not alone — Indiana law now protects you from summer shutoffs during extreme heat if you are enrolled in LIHEAP, and new utility assistance programs are required under HEA 1002; contact your local Community Action Agency today to apply for the Energy Assistance Program and learn your rights before disconnection threatens your safety.

Rate increases continue to outpace inflation and disproportionately burden residential customers over large commercial users — enact enforceable affordability standards tied to household income, expand LIHEAP enrollment outreach, and ensure the IURC has clear performance metrics and accountability mechanisms under HEA 1002 that translate into measurable bill relief.

Indiana has gone from having some of the lowest electric bills in the nation to the 12th highest, with NIPSCO customers seeing approximately a 16.75% rate increase phased in beginning July 2025, Duke Energy customers facing a $295.7 million annual revenue increase, and AES Indiana customers now facing a new $71 million increase — the cumulative cost of these rate cases falls hardest on everyday Hoosier households.

Apply HEA 1002's performance-based ratemaking tools rigorously: use quarterly utility data on arrears, disconnects, and burden rates to hold utilities accountable, and treat affordability — not just infrastructure investment recovery — as a primary regulatory standard in every pending and future rate proceeding.

rate_cases.description: Duke Energy Indiana's first base rate case since 2019 requested approximately $491.5 million (16.2%) in annual revenue increases. The IURC approved approximately $295.7 million in additional annual revenues in a phased implementation, and the OUCC filed in June 2026 alleging Duke has since over-collected more than $89 million beyond what was authorized.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approximately $295.7 million annual revenue increase (~9–10% effective increase for residential customers); OUCC alleges an additional unauthorized ~$86–90 million per year over-collection

rate_cases.description: AES Indiana filed a regulatory rate review in June 2025 requesting approximately $193–200 million in increased revenues (13.5% over two phases), citing rising operational costs and infrastructure investments. The IURC voted 3-1 on June 17, 2026 to approve $71 million — roughly 37% of the original request — to be phased in starting July 2026, prompting Governor Braun to call for a rehearing.
rate_cases.rate_change: $71 million annual revenue increase; approximately $5 per month for a typical residential customer using 1,000 kWh, phased in over two steps in 2026 and early 2027

rate_cases.utility: NIPSCO (Northern Indiana Public Service Company) — Electric
rate_cases.description: NIPSCO filed an electric rate case initially seeking $369 million in additional annual revenues; a settlement filed February 2025 reduced the request to approximately $257 million. The IURC approved the settlement in full on June 26, 2025, with rates phased in beginning July 2025 through early 2026, resulting in approximately $23 per month more for an average residential customer using 672 kWh — a 16.75% increase.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approximately $257 million annual revenue increase; ~$23/month (16.75%) for typical residential customer

rate_cases.utility: NIPSCO (Northern Indiana Public Service Company) — Gas Metering Investigation
rate_cases.description: Following NIPSCO's rollout of Advanced Metering Infrastructure, the IURC opened an investigation in November 2025 after NIPSCO admitted that more than 3,500 natural gas customers had been billed double their actual usage due to incorrectly installed mechanical meters. NIPSCO is issuing refund credits of 50% of the prior 12 months of overbilled amounts and correcting all meters by end of 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: No rate change; customers overbilled double actual usage receiving partial refund credits

rate_cases.description: CenterPoint Electric filed for a $118.8 million (16.02%) base rate increase, including a proposed monthly fixed charge increase to $23.20. An order was issued and new rates took effect in phases through 2025; CenterPoint's Indiana president committed to stable rates through 2027.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approximately $118.8 million annual revenue increase (16.02%); fixed monthly charge raised significantly

rate_cases.description: The OUCC filed a petition in June 2026 alleging Duke Energy Indiana has over-collected more than $89 million from ratepayers since the 2025 rate order, and is on track to over-collect an additional $86.5 million per year, because rates were implemented with footnote 'refinements' not originally authorized by the IURC. IURC Commissioners Zay and Seyfried rejected the OUCC's arguments in a May 2026 filing, concluding Duke was complying with its rate order.
rate_cases.rate_change: Alleged unauthorized over-collection of ~$89 million past and ~$86.5 million annually going forward

articles.headline: State regulators approve $70M increase for AES, less than utility originally sought
articles.summary: The IURC voted 3-1 on June 17, 2026 to approve a $71 million electricity rate increase for AES Indiana customers — about 37% of the utility's initial $193 million request — to be phased in beginning July 2026. Governor Braun expressed 'deep disappointment' and called for a rehearing, while the OUCC and Citizens Action Coalition criticized the increase as falling disproportionately on residential customers.

articles.headline: Indiana's ratepayer advocate accuses Duke Energy Indiana of over-collecting $90 million from customers
articles.summary: The Indiana OUCC filed a petition in early June 2026 alleging that Duke Energy Indiana has collected more than $89 million beyond what the IURC authorized in its January 2025 rate order, and that the error will result in an additional $86.5 million over-collected each year going forward. IURC Commissioners Zay and Seyfried had separately concluded Duke was in compliance, creating a dispute between the agency's presiding officers and the ratepayer advocate.

articles.headline: Indiana now blocks utility shutoffs on the hottest days — but only for some customers
articles.summary: Under a new Indiana law (HEA 1002), privately and cooperatively owned electric utilities are prohibited from disconnecting customers within 48 hours of a forecasted heat index of 95 degrees or higher — but only if the customer is enrolled in the federal LIHEAP energy assistance program. Consumer advocates noted the protection is narrower than proposed, leaving many low-income households that haven't enrolled in LIHEAP without coverage.

articles.headline: Gov. Braun says new law will protect Indiana utility customers from rising bills
articles.summary: Governor Mike Braun ceremonially signed HEA 1002 at the Statehouse on April 6, 2026, calling it landmark legislation to overhaul utility regulation and put affordability first. The law requires performance-based multi-year rate plans, mandates low-income customer assistance programs, and adds summer disconnection protections tied to heat index forecasts.

articles.headline: 'Big five' Indiana utilities defend rates in regulator questioning
articles.summary: The IURC held a daylong Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability on March 24, 2026, in Indianapolis, during which executives from AES Indiana, CenterPoint Energy, Duke Energy Indiana, Indiana Michigan Power, and NIPSCO defended their rate structures before commissioners. AES Indiana President Brandi Davis-Handy noted that residential bills have largely tracked inflation over time, while CenterPoint's Indiana president touted a commitment to stable rates through 2027.

articles.headline: Indiana utility regulators call in 'big five' energy providers for affordability investigation
articles.summary: IURC Chairman Andy Zay announced on February 25, 2026 that the commission would conduct an unprecedented Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability, calling in representatives from all five major investor-owned utilities. Citizens Action Coalition called the move 'long overdue,' and IURC stated it would follow with a statewide listening tour to hear directly from ratepayers.

articles.headline: NIPSCO admits to overcharging more than 3,500 customers after new metering technology rollout
articles.summary: Newly unsealed regulatory records revealed that NIPSCO's rollout of Advanced Metering Infrastructure caused old mechanical meters to be installed incorrectly, resulting in 3,542 customers being billed double their actual natural gas usage. NIPSCO is issuing refund credits equal to 50% of the prior 12 months of charges and has pledged to correct all affected meters by end of 2026.

articles.headline: AES Indiana customers will see a temporary increase in bills this summer
articles.summary: AES Indiana notified customers that a fuel adjustment charge of $3.98 per month (for 1,000 kWh users) will be applied from June through August 2026, due to increased fuel costs including from Winter Storm Fern in January 2026. This temporary tracker charge comes on top of the permanent $71 million base rate increase approved by the IURC on June 17, 2026.

articles.headline: ICV, CAC Unveil Policy Plan to Lower Energy Costs for Hoosiers
articles.source: Indiana Conservation Voters / Citizens Action Coalition
articles.summary: Indiana Conservation Voters and Citizens Action Coalition jointly released the 'Help Hoosiers Now: Ratepayer Relief Plan,' a package of short- and long-term policy proposals to address Indiana's worsening energy affordability crisis. The plan includes prohibiting utilities from passing unnecessary costs to ratepayers, expanding low-income protections, and requiring transparency on disconnection and arrears data.

articles.headline: How Indy's rising electricity bills stack up
articles.summary: Axios Indianapolis examined how AES Indiana's proposed 13.5% rate increase — which was ultimately trimmed to a $71 million approval — compares to residential electricity bills across the Midwest, noting that Indiana has transitioned from among the lowest-cost states to the 12th highest electric bills nationally. The OUCC was simultaneously conducting its broader affordability investigation into all five investor-owned utilities.

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chairman
stakeholders.quote: "Indiana, like every state, is facing a real challenge when it comes to energy affordability. Costs are increasing across the board, and rising utility bills are placing added pressure on budgets that, for many, are already stretched thin." (Direct quote)
stakeholders.source: IURC press release / Fox 59 / WEVV, February 25, 2026

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chairman
stakeholders.quote: "In my short time as chairman of the IURC, my fellow Commissioners and I have heard from thousands of utility customers from all over Indiana that they are struggling to pay their energy bills." (Direct quote)
stakeholders.source: IURC news release on affordability listening sessions / Daily Journal, March 23, 2026

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: "The AES rate order issued today is an outrage and Hoosiers deserve better! Governor Braun has made it clear that ratepayer affordability is a priority, far more than just a 'hot topic.'" (Direct quote)
stakeholders.source: OUCC press statement / Indiana Public Media (IPM), June 18, 2026

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: "AES Indiana has not demonstrated sufficient evidence for an increase in this rate case, which it filed less than 14 months after nabbing approval in a previous rate case." (Direct quote)
stakeholders.source: OUCC news release / Indiana Capital Chronicle, September 10, 2025

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "For too long, Hoosiers have been footing the bill for decisions they didn't make and policies they didn't ask for. Indiana's regulatory system should be built to protect consumers, but that goal can only be achieved if lawmakers give regulators the tools and authority it needs. The solutions have been ready for years. The need is now incredibly urgent." (Direct quote)
stakeholders.source: ICV/CAC joint press release on Ratepayer Relief Plan, February 1, 2026

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "It is a shame that it had to get to this point before they took action. It is long overdue. Ratepayers from across the state are struggling." (Direct quote, referring to IURC's affordability inquiry announcement)

stakeholders.name: Ben Inskeep
stakeholders.title: Program Director
stakeholders.quote: "We've heard a lot of rhetoric from Governor [Mike] Braun and the commission about affordability, but unfortunately we didn't see that carried through with the actions today." (Direct quote, reacting to the IURC's June 17 approval of the AES rate increase)
stakeholders.source: WTHR Channel 13 / Citizens Action Coalition statement, June 18, 2026

stakeholders.name: Ben Inskeep
stakeholders.title: Program Director
stakeholders.quote: "Residential ratepayers will bear the brunt of this increase which is contrary to [Braun's] stated priority of more affordable utility bills." (Direct quote, on the AES Indiana rate order)
stakeholders.source: Citizens Action Coalition statement on X (Twitter) / Daily Journal, June 18, 2026

stakeholders.name: Brian Latham
stakeholders.title: Electric Division Director
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: AES Indiana's case-in-chief "appears to simply give lip service to affordability while focusing on shareholder returns," and noted that residential customers pay 25% more in 2025 than they did in 2023. (Direct quote from regulatory testimony)
stakeholders.source: OUCC testimony filed in Cause No. 46258 / WFYI / Indiana Capital Chronicle, September 10, 2025

stakeholders.name: Mike Braun
stakeholders.title: Governor
stakeholders.quote: "My top priority is affordability, which is why I am deeply disappointed by the IURC's approval of another AES rate increase. Yesterday's decision by the IURC to allow another rate increase by AES is unacceptable. I am calling on the Office of Utility Consumer Counselor to petition for a reconsideration and rehearing of this case." (Direct quote)
stakeholders.source: Governor's official statement / Indiana Capital Chronicle / NWI Times, June 18–19, 2026

stakeholders.name: Brandi Davis-Handy
stakeholders.title: President
stakeholders.quote: AES Indiana has "maintained some of the lowest rates in the state for more than a decade through disciplined planning and a focus on efficiency." (Direct quote from AES Indiana statement following IURC rate order)
stakeholders.source: Indiana Public Media (IPM) / Indiana Capital Chronicle, June 18, 2026
